title: 福爾摩沙領事史溫侯致信葛雷博士之信件節錄
alternative: Extracts from letters recently addressed by Mr. R. Swinhoe, H.B.M. Consul in Formosa, to Dr. J.E. Gray
description: 史溫侯寫信介紹福爾摩沙的哺乳類，包含大靈貓（Viverra ashtoni, n. sp，目前學名Viverra zibetha）、臺灣獼猴（Macacus cyclopis）、臺灣黑熊、臺灣雲豹（Leopardus brachyurus）、白鼻心（Paguma larvata）、蝙蝠科（Vespertilionidae）、野豬（Porcula taivana，目前學名Sus scrofa）、歐亞水獺（Lutra nair，目前學名 Lutra lutra）、臺灣穿山甲（Manis brachyurus，目前學名 Manis pentadactyla pentadactyla？）、臭鼩（Sorex myorurus，中國麝鼠）、溝鼠 （Mus decumanus）、家鼷鼠（Mus musculus，家鼠）、臺灣刺鼠（Mus coninga，目前學名Niviventer coninga）。

relation.name: Reed Digital Collections:Formosa Nineteenth Century Images

moreInfo.title: 來源
moreInfo.description: Swinhoe, R.  "Extracts from letters recently addressed by Mr. R. Swinhoe, H.B.M. Consul in Formosa, to Dr. J.E. Gray."  Proceedings of the Zoological Society of London (1864): 378-383.  [Letters dated Foochow, 27 July 1864; Tamsuy, 10 August 1864; Formosa (Takow?) 5 September 1864.]（羅伯特・史溫侯。〈福爾摩沙領事史溫侯致信葛雷博士之信件節錄〉，《倫敦動物學會集刊》1864年刊第378-383頁。1864年7月27日撰於福州；1864年8月10日撰於淡水，1864年9月5日撰於福爾摩沙（打狗？）。）

languageDocuments.text: ##--SPLIT--##    The Secretary read the following extracts from letters recently addressed by Mr. R. Swinhoe, H. B. M. Consul in Formosa, to Dr. J. E. Gray:—    “Foochow, 27th July, 1864.    “Having been cruising about the Formosan Channel for the last month, I have arrived at last at Foochow; and being detained at this port a few days, I have managed to find leisure to make a few notes in zoology. The only strange mammal that has yet occurred here is a Porcupine (Hystrix, sp.?). It was killed before I got here, and unfortunately not preserved; I regret, therefore, that I have no remarks to offer as to its appearance. The people who shot it are positive that it was wild, though I strongly suspect it was brought up from Singapore in a junk. At Amoy I heard of some Hedgehogs (Erinaceus) having been offered for sale, but of what species I could not ascertain. They were said to have been taken in the vicinity. A gentleman here has two magnificent skins from Newchwang, the northernmost part of China. One is that of a Tiger, the Chinese race, pale, with few stripes; and the other that of a Leopard, evidently your Leopardus japonensis. I strongly suspect that the animal you procured with a Japanese stamp was a skin procured by the Japanese in their trading-stations. No Leopard is said by the Japanese to inhabit the islands of Japan.    “I am about to remove from Tamsuy to Takow, S.W. Formosa, very shortly. I am closing up and sending home all my collections, and will send you a memoir on the new mammals added to the For-     ##--SPLIT--##    mosan list; but this must wait till my return to that wild and solitary isle.    “Viverra ashtoni, n. sp. Shot at Suykaou, up the River Min, Foochow, by Mr. Tk. K. Ashton, and kindly lent by that gentleman to me for description. The skin is preserved flat, and the claws have been, unfortunately, cut away. It is the largest of the genus I have seen, and is quite a beautiful animal. Total length 4 feet; tail 16 inches, heavy, bushy, and handsome, slightly tapering. Its ground-colour may be described as light ochreous white, finely mottled with black; blacker on the occiput, hind neck, fore shoulders, fore thighs, and along the back. The mottling effect is produced by some of the hairs having black tips, some being entirely black in the deeper parts. The downy fur or pile at the roots of the hairs is brownish ash-grey. The paws are blackish brown, spotted with a deeper hue, the spots being carried up towards the thighs. Muzzle with a white patch on each side; bars moderate, with a black spot on their outward bases. A united bar occurs behind the ears, and runs in a line down the side of the neck, curving downwards before reaching the shoulder, and nearly meeting on the under neck; another crosses the lower throat, and another disconnected one twists about on either side of the neck below the first line. The neck is more decidedly white, edged with ochreous. From the nuchal bar     ##--SPLIT--##    a black list begins to show, which takes a more certain form between the shoulders and runs down the back into the third dark bar of the tail. The hairs of this list are so thickly set, and so much longer than the rest of the fur, that they look as if added on by art. The tail has a pale indistinct bar at its roots, and four others along its length, the latter increasing in breadth and blackness as they approach the tip; they are stretched lozenge-shaped across the tail, being broader at their middles than at their sides. The rest of the tail is white, forming with the tip five bars. Before the eyes to the nose brown; moustache-bristles long and white, a few of the upper ones being brown.    “The animal does not appear to be well known in this neighbourhood, the present being the only instance of its having been observed and preserved by Europeans. I have taken its specific name from its discoverer, Mr. Ashton.”“Tamsuy, 10th August, 1864.    “As I am now closing my collecting-operation in North Formosa, it would be as well to send you a few remarks on the more recent experience I have been able to acquire. The Monkey (Macacus cyclopis) is distributed throughout the hilly country. We saw a large party of them the other day at Lungkeaou.    “The Formosan Bear, I feel sure, is an undescribed animal. I have not been able to procure a perfect skin, but I procured some paws lately at Sawo Bay, and the cap of a savage covered with a patch of its skin. It is intermediate in size between the Bears of Japan and Thibet. It is about four feet and a half from muzzle to tail, and is clothed with rigid, somewhat short, black hair, and carries in the adult a crescent-shaped white mark on its breast between the fore legs. In the young this patch would appear to be brown; at least the Indian’s cap above referred to had the mark on it of that colour. Of the paws, the fore paw measures, from the carpal joint to the end of the middle claw, 5 inches; its greatest breadth above is 3 inches: claws well hooked, and cultrated underneath: palm-pad nearly triangular, the apex towards the carpus, inclining outwardly; length 21/2 inches, greatest breadth 26/10: each of the toes has bare oval pads underneath. Hind foot 6 inches in length measured underneath, greatest breadth above 26/10; sole-pad an irregular triangle, 36/10 long, 21/2 greatest breadth; the pad of the outer and inner toes joining with the sole-pad, the rest of the toe-pads, as those of the fore foot, having cushions of hair between. Toe-pads of hind foot smaller than those of fore foot; claws not so long as in fore foot, and more covered with hair, being nearly hidden by it. For the Formosan Bear may I take the liberty of proposing the name Ursus formosanus? I have heard objections made to Formosa being formed into an adjective by the termination anus; is not this liberty sanctioned by classical Latin in such a word as Augustani being applied to the people of the cities bearing the name Augusta?    “A few words now on Leopardus brachyurus. I have not seen this animal alive. As Sawo I procured two skins of adult animals     ##--SPLIT--##    of this species, and at Lungkeaou (S.W. Formosa) one of an apparently younger specimen. They all show smaller heads than in Wolf’s figure (P. Z. S. 1862, p. 347). All these skins have short tails, that of the Lungkeaou specimen not being ripped open; so that there can be no doubt of the distinctness of the species in that character from the L. macrocelis. This Lungkeaou specimen is lighter than those from Sawo in the tawny hue of its upper parts, and its pale under parts are nearly white; the streaks on its back are not so broad, and do not so frequently coalesce; its face is more narrowly and distinctly streaked, and its tail is more narrowly and numerously banded. The ears of all are black behind, with a light spot low down. Their moustache-bristles are some black and some white. The Lungkeaou skin is the only one that has legs. The feet appear to have been small, and the spots on the sides of the legs small and distinct.Sawo skin	 Length 2ft. 9 in., tail 22 1/4 in.Sawo skin, No. 2	 Length 3ft. 0 in., tail 25 in.	Lungkeaou skin	 Length 2ft. 9 in., tail 30 3/4 in.	    “Paguma larvata.—I have received further specimens. The ears were in all small and round, as I described them; but the tail varies in length, and in the greater and less amount of black along its length.    “Vespertilionidæ.—Most of the Bats that fly about of an evening are small, and, I think, of the same species as those procured at Taiwanfoo. I have, however, a couple of specimens of a small Leaf-nose, taken in the cave at Kelung, which will be interesting. The other day I observed a species (of Pteropus?) of a bright colour, about 8 inches long, hanging by the heels on a large leaf. I, unfortunately, had no gun with me, and, trying to knock them down with my stick, missed him. The Chinese are very unsuccessful collectors of Bats, and very rarely bring me examples.    “Porcula taivana.—I have not managed to procure or get a sight of an adult. The largest I have seen was perhaps half-grown, in which the stripes and spots still remained. The natives insist that the animal is so marked all its life. I have purchased several young ones, with the view of sending them to the Society, but have not succeeded in inducing them to live. One lived with me for over a month. I took it to Foochow with the hope of getting it off all safe, but it died the day after my arrival at that place. A half-grown Pig of this species was bought by a friend here. It was very wild, and always jumped furiously at any person that came near its pen. It was covered with rather long bristles, longest down the back, and had a white tail. I despair, for the present, of getting a specimen in this vicinity; but I may meet with it again in the south. I will now enumerate the additions that I have made to my first list of eighteen species of Mammals of Formosa (P. Z. S. 1862, p. 347).    “19. Lutra nair, with white sides to its neck. An indifferent skin seen at Sawo, in the hands of Chinese there; doubtless killed on the island.    “20. Manis brachyurus? When I was in Takow, in 1861, I ##--SPLIT--##    heard from the natives of the existence of this beast in the island; and in Dutch books on Formosa I saw it spoken of as the ‘Taewan Devil,’ the old Hollanders, like the Chinese, not being able to divine to what class of the animal kingdom it belonged. I have succeeded in procuring a specimen, and am sending it home. It is almost sure to be the South China species, Manis brachyurus; but, not having specimens to compare with it, I cannot set this down as a certainty.	“21. Sorex——, sp.? I found a nest of this under a decayed patch of buffalo-dung, and secured two small Shrews; they are small and immature, and closely affine to the foregoing, but destitute of the must-odour, and, I think, of a different species. I am sending them home for determination.	“23. Mus decumanus. Very common, and rather variable in colour.	“24. Mus musculus (the House-Mouse). I have seen, in houses here, little creatures which I have taken to be of this species.	“25. Mus coninga, Swinhoe, P. Z. S. 1864, p. 185. This species does not affect trees, like the M. flavescens of China.	“26. Mus ——? About one-third the size of the common Rat, and of rather similar colour, with softer fur and small feet. From the interior. I was intending to give a full description of this apparently new species, but I find my specimens have been already packed and forwarded to M. Verreaux.	“27. Mus ——? With reddish-brown back, interspersed with black hairs, whitish under parts, large head, and rounded ears. I picked up a mutilated specimen on a hill not far off. It is almost twice as big as a common Mouse, with longer tail. It appears to be a field species, and it probably undescribed. I have, unfortunately, not been able to procure good specimens.	“This is all I have at present to offer on the Mammals.	“I have been of late rather assiduous in collecting land shells. From this neighbourhood I have got nearly thirty species, from South Formosa four, and from Foochow two. The South Formosan, comprising three Helices and one Bulimus, are all distinct from those we meet with here. But both the Foochow species have close allies in Kelung, differing only enough perhaps to note them as varieties. But my knowledge of shells is so limited that I must refrain from talking too wildly of species and varieties, and leave those nice distinctions to the deeper learning of Mr. Cuming and Dr. Pfeiffer.	“I trust these notes will interest you. They may be worth communicating to the Zoological Society. You will probably not hear from me again until I have made some progress in Takow (South Formosa).”	“With regard to my report on the Formosan Mammalia, it is necessary to make some corrections with regard to the supposed Porcula. Hitherto I had only received very young specimens, and, ##--SPLIT--##relying on the reports of the natives as to its carrying its stripes at a mature age, I was led to suppose the animal a second species of Hodgson’s genus Porcula. I have, however, lately had four live wild Pigs brought to me, which, judging from their appearance, I should take to be from five to six months old; and in this estimate I am borne out by the fact that it is during March and the commencement of April that sucklings are brought to us by the country-people. Two of the four pigs sulked and died; a third battered himself to death; and the fourth was very unmanageable, and eventually showed an inclination to droop, so that I was obliged to have him killed. In their grunts, squeaks, and other noises emitted, they were entirely porcine. The largest is about 21/2  feet from snout to rump, and about 11/2 foot high at the shoulder; tail 23/4 inches long, with a further 3/4 inch length of hair at tip. The undeveloped state of their teeth and skulls generally proclaim their juvenility, so that the mature animal would be probably twice as large at least. The distinct bands have disappeared, and their incisors project in the usual manner of typical pigs. They are therefore wrongly referred to Porcula, but more properly belong to true Sus. I have three skins and three skulls. The general colour of the upper parts is yellowish brown, thickly mixed with black hairs, which give the fur a mottled appearance. A patch just before the shoulder, and another on the forehead before the eyes, are paler and more free from black hairs. The black hairs are longest on the back of the neck and on the rump. The under parts and tail are white, more or less mixed with black hairs. The chin is dark fuscous. A triangular line of white runs away from the angle of the mouth, bounded on each side by a line of black hairs. This last character calls to my mind the peculiar characteristic of the Japanese Wild Pig (Sus leucomystax); and indeed they may be of that species, which is probably also the Wild Pig of South China, if it be true that the Domestic Chinese Hog is descended from that wild stock. The appearance of these animals would doubtless have altered with advancing age; but they may be sufficiently stamped to indicate the species. I have no copy of the ‘Fauna Japonica’ with me to refer to. I am sending the specimens home, and will leave you to settle the question of their identity or otherwise with the Wild Japanese Pig. I may eventually succeed in getting a full-grown animal, and may perhaps manage to get live specimens home for the Gardens. I have no pigs’ skulls to compare with those of our species. There is, however, a drawing of those of Sus scrofa ferus and Sus pliciceps illustrating a paper by you in the ‘Proceedings.’ The skull of Sus taivanus is at once distinguishable from either of these by its well-rounded forehead, and by the sides of the parietal and temporal bones being well convex instead of concave. But it is not, of course with Sus scrofa that this has to be compared, as, from the external appearance of the skins, there is not the least chance of our pig being referable to that species.”

##--SPLIT--##    秘書長閱讀近期來信並摘錄如下，來自羅伯特‧史溫侯，福爾摩沙領事，致約翰‧愛德華‧格雷博士1。    1864年7月27日，福州    上個月，我乘著船在福爾摩沙海峽到處航行，最後終於抵達福州。在這個港口滯被留了幾天之後，我終於找到空閒時間來做一些動物學方面的筆記。這裡唯一尚未出現的奇怪哺乳動物是豪豬（Hyatrix sp. ?）。在我抵達這裡之前，牠就被殺死了，更不幸的是，標本沒有保存下來。因此，很抱歉，對於牠的外觀形態，我沒有任何評論。射殺牠的人表示牠肯定是野生的，雖然我強烈懷疑牠是從新加坡隨著帆船船一起被運過來的。在廈門，我聽說有人要出售刺猬（Erinaceus），但我不確定是哪一種刺蝟。據說牠們是在附近被帶走的。這裡有一位先生擁有兩張來自中國最北端的牛庄【Newchwang，現今的遼寧省營口市】的壯觀毛皮。一張是中國的老虎，體色白皙，僅有少量的條紋。另一隻則是豹，顯然是華北豹（Leopardus japonensis，目前學名 Panthera pardus japonensis）。我強烈懷疑您拿到的打上日本戳印的標本是日本人在其貿易中心(trading-stations)購買的毛皮。日本人說日本列島上沒有任何豹的存在。    我很快就要從淡水（Tamsuy）搬到福爾摩沙島西南部的打狗（Takow，現今高雄市）。 我要打包所有的收藏並將它們寄回家，我還會寄給您一篇報告，是有關新加入福爾摩沙物種名錄中的一些哺乳動物；
    ---------------1 譯註：全名John Edward Gray，19世紀英國動物學家，1833年與其他人共同創立英國皇家昆蟲學會（Royal Entomological Society），1840-1874年擔任大英博物館動物學部門。    ##--SPLIT--##    但是這必須等到我回到那個蠻荒又孤寂的小島。    大靈貓（Viverra ashtoni, n. sp，目前學名Viverra zibetha）。 是阿什頓（T. K. Ashton）先生在福州閩江上游的Suykao2射殺的，是阿什頓先生借給我以進行描述。牠的毛皮保持平整，可惜爪子被切掉了。牠是我所見過這種屬的物種裡面最大的個體，也是一種很漂亮的動物。全長4英呎、尾巴16英吋、相當重、有濃密的毛、美麗，略呈錐形。牠的底色可以說是是淡赭白色，帶有黑色斑點。枕骨、後頸、前肩、前大腿和背部的斑點顏色較黑。會出現黑色斑點是因為一些毛的末端為黑色，其中有些位於深處的毛是全黑的。毛髮基部的絨毛為棕灰色。腳掌是黑褐色，帶有較深色調的斑點，這些斑點朝大腿方向延伸。吻端兩側各有一個白色斑塊；底部具有深淺適中的條紋，其外側各有一個黑點。耳朵後方有條相連的寬帶，沿頸部一側成一條線延伸，向下彎曲，直到到達肩部，幾乎在頸部下方會合。另一條通過喉嚨下方，還有一條未相連的條紋纏繞在第一條條紋下方的頸部兩側。牠的頸部明顯呈白色，帶有赭色邊緣。一黑色條紋從頸項

---------------2 譯註：不確定地名。    ##--SPLIT--##    開始延伸，在肩膀之間越來越明顯，並一直向後延伸到尾巴的第三條黑色寬帶。這條黑色條紋中的毛髮非常濃密，比其餘毛髮的長度還要長得多，讓它們看起來好像是額外添加的裝飾品。尾巴的基部有一條蒼白模糊的寬帶條紋，沿著尾巴還有另外四條，越接近末端的寬帶時，越寬且越黑。這些寬帶環繞著尾巴，呈現拉長的菱形狀，中間的部分比側兩側寬。尾巴的其餘部分為白色，於末端形成五條寬帶。雙眼前到鼻部為褐色；鬍鬚長且呈白色，上側的鬍鬚有些為褐色。    這種動物在這一帶似乎並不為人所知，目前唯一的案例是歐洲人所觀察和保存的標本。我使用的學名是來自發現者阿什頓先生的命名。1864年8月10日，淡水    由於我即將結束福爾摩沙島北部的採集活動，我也想就我最近的經驗發表一些看法。 臺灣獼猴（Macacus cyclopis）分布於整個丘陵地區。前幾天，我們在瑯橋【Lungkeaou，現今恆春鎮】發現了一大群的猴群。    我敢肯定，臺灣黑熊是一種尚不為人知的動物。我一直沒能取得一張完整的毛皮，但最近我在蘇澳灣（Sawo Bay）得到一些熊爪，以及一個番人（savage）的帽子，這頂帽子上蓋有一塊黑熊的毛皮。牠的大小介於日本熊和西藏棕熊之間。從吻端到尾巴大約四英呎半，身上有剛硬略短的黑毛。成年個體前肢之間的胸口上帶有新月形的白色斑塊。在年輕個體身上，這個斑塊看起來呈褐色。至少前面提到的土著帽子（Indian ' s cap）上的毛皮，上面的斑塊是這個顏色。熊爪方面，前爪的長度從腕關節到中爪末端長5英吋。其最大寬度為3英吋；爪鉤明顯，刃面鋒利而尖銳；熊掌的肉墊接近三角形，尖端朝向腕骨，向外延伸。長度21/2英吋，最大寬度26/10英吋；每個腳趾下方都有裸露的橢圓形肉墊。後肢長6英吋，最大寬度超過25/10英吋；腳底肉墊呈不規則三角形，長35/10英吋，最大寬度21/2 英吋；外腳趾和內腳趾的肉墊與腳底相連接，其餘的腳趾肉墊和前腳一樣，腳趾之間長有毛髮。後腳的腳趾肉墊比前腳小；後肢的爪子也不像前腳那麼長，而且被更多毛髮蓋住，幾乎被隱藏起來。對於臺灣黑熊，我可以冒昧提議建議使用Ursus formosanus這個名字嗎？我聽到有人反對將Formosa加上anus 結尾變成形容詞形態。難道這種自由不是由古典拉丁文所認可的嗎？例如Augustani一詞不就是用來指稱那些叫作奧古斯塔（Augusta）的城市中的人民嗎？    現在我們來談談臺灣雲豹（Leopardus brachyurus），我還沒有看過活生生的雲豹。我在    ##--SPLIT--##    買了兩張成年雲豹的毛皮，在瑯嶠（福爾摩沙西南部）獲得一隻顯然比較年輕的標本。牠們的頭部都比沃爾夫3所畫的還要小（1862年《倫敦動物學會集刊》第347頁）。這些毛皮標本都是短尾，而瑯嶠獲得的標本沒有被割開。因此，毫無疑問，該物種在這項特徵上與巽他雲豹（L. macrocelis，目前學名 Neofelis diardi）不同。瑯嶠標本體背的茶褐色調，比蘇澳的標本較淡，而腹部及四肢內側的淡色調幾乎呈現白色。背部的條紋不那麼寬，大多沒有接合在一起。牠的臉部更窄且有明顯的條紋，尾巴更細且帶有更多環紋。所有標本的耳朵後方都是黑色的，下面有個淡色斑點。鬍鬚的鬃毛有些為黑色、有些則為白色。瑯嶠的毛皮標本是唯一有四肢的標本。 牠的腳似乎很小，腿兩側的斑點小但十分明顯。蘇澳毛皮標本 ……. 長 2 英呎 9 英吋，尾長 22 1/4 英吋。蘇澳毛皮標本2號.. 長 3 英呎 0 英吋，尾長 25 英吋。瑯嶠毛皮標本 ……. 長 2 英呎 9 英吋，尾長 30 3/4 英吋。    白鼻心（Paguma larvata）：我後來又收到了更多標本。正如我所描述過的，耳朵全都又小又圓。但在尾巴的長度以及尾巴表面黑色的分佈上差異相當大。    蝙蝠科（Vespertilionidae）：在晚上飛行的蝙蝠大多是小型蝙蝠，我認為牠們和我在臺灣府（Taiwan foo）取得的蝙蝠是相同品種。不過，我有一些在基隆（Kelung）的山洞裡採集到的小型葉鼻蝠標本，這些標本很有意思。前幾天，我發現了一種長約8英吋、顏色鮮豔的蝙蝠（Pteropus?，狐蝠屬），用腳後跟將自己倒掛在一片大葉子上。可惜的是，我沒有帶槍，所以試著用棍子將牠們打下來，但是沒打到。中國人非常不會抓蝙蝠，很少帶標本給我。    野豬（Porcula taivana，目前學名Sus scrofa）：我一直沒辦法取得成年個體的標本，也還沒看過。我見過最大的個體，大概也是發育到一半的個體，身上都還有條紋和斑點。當地人（The natives）堅持這種動物終生都有這些紋路。我已經買到了幾隻幼體，打算把牠們送回皇家動物學會，但是都沒成功讓牠們活下來。其中一隻我養了一個多月。我把牠帶到福州，希望平安地把牠運走，但是牠在我抵達的第二天就死了。 這裡的友人買了一隻這種豬的亞成個體。牠非常狂野，只要有任何靠近人牠的圍欄，牠就會怒氣沖沖地撲向他們。牠全身被相當長的鬃毛覆蓋，最長的鬃毛在背部，有一條白色的尾巴。目前，我已經對在附近取得標本一事不抱希望，但是到了南部之後可能會再有機會看到這個物種。現在，我將列舉我在之前第一份福爾摩沙島的18種哺乳動物名錄（1862年《倫敦動物學會集刊》第347頁）中所加入的一些新物種。    19. 歐亞水獺（Lutra nair，目前學名 Lutra lutra），頸部側面為白色。在蘇澳有張普通的毛皮標本，是在中國人手上看到； 這只標本毫無疑問是在島上被殺。    20. 臺灣穿山甲（Manis brachyurus，目前學名 Manis pentadactyla pentadactyla？）1861年，當我在打狗（Takow）時，

---------------3 譯註：Wolf，這裡是指喬瑟夫‧沃爾夫（Joseph Wolf）。        ##--SPLIT--##    從當地人（the natives）那裡聽說過島上有這種動物。在一些有關福爾摩沙島的荷蘭文書中，我看到牠被稱為「臺灣惡魔（Taewan Devil）」，古老的荷蘭人也像中國人一樣，無法判斷牠在動物界的分類歸屬。我已經順利取得一件標本，並寄回家中。此物種幾乎可以肯定是中國南部的「中華穿山甲（Manis brachyurus）」。但是因為沒有其他標本可以與之比較，所以這一點我還無法確認。    21. 臭鼩（Sorex myorurus，中國麝鼠4）在淡水中國人的房舍中並不罕見。    22. 某種囓齒類？我在腐爛的水牛糞便下找到一個巢穴，並逮到了兩隻小型鼩鼱。牠們很小且尚未性成熟，與上述的物種很相似，但是身上沒有那種氣味，所以我認為應該是另外一種物種。 我正將牠們寄回英國確認。    23. 溝鼠 （Mus decumanus）。非常常見的物種，顏色十分多樣。    24. 家鼷鼠（Mus musculus，家鼠）。 我在這裡的屋子裡，看過一些我認為屬於該物種的小傢伙。    25. 臺灣刺鼠（Mus coninga，目前學名Niviventer coninga）。1864年《倫敦動物集刊》第185頁。這種物種不會危害樹木，就像中國的玄鼠（M. flavescens，目前學名 Rattus rattus）。    26 某種囓齒類？體型大約是一般老鼠的三分之一大小，顏色相當類似，但體毛較軟，腳較小。此物種來自內陸。這個物種顯然是新種，我原本打算做一個完整的描述，但是我發現我的標本已經打包好並寄給了韋羅先生（M. Verreaux）5了。    27 某種囓齒類？背面紅褐色，夾雜著黑色的毛，腹面泛白色，頭部大而耳朵呈圓形。我在不遠的小丘陵地上撿到一件殘缺的標本。其大小幾乎是普通鼠類的兩倍，尾巴更長。似乎是一種野生的物種，可能尚未被描述過。不幸的是，我還無法取得品質良好的標本。    以上就是我目前所能提供的所有關於哺乳動物的描述。    我最近很努力地在收集蝸牛。在這附近，我蒐集了將近三十種蝸牛。其中有四件標本來自福爾摩沙島南部，來自福州的則有兩件。 來自福爾摩沙島南部的標本，包括三件蝸牛屬（Helix）和一件螺屬（Bulimus），都和我們這裡的物種明顯不同。但是，兩件福州的標本都有分布於基隆的近緣物種，牠們彼此之間的差異可能僅足以將牠們記錄為不同的變種。但是，我對貝類的了解非常有限，所以於我必須克制自己不要過度地談論物種和變種，而應將這些細微的區分留給庫名先生（Cuming）和普發博士（Pfeiffer）進行更深入的研究。    我相信您會對這些筆記有興趣，可能值得將它們寄給倫敦動物學會。在我在打狗（福爾摩沙南部）獲得一些進展以前，您應該不會再收到我的信。    1864年9月5日，福爾摩沙島    我對福爾摩沙哺乳動物所做的報告，有必要對姬豬屬（Porcula）做一些更正。 目前為止我收到的標本，都是很年輕的個體，

---------------4 譯註：鼠類的學名變遷較複雜，目前學名應是Suncus murinus。5 譯註：全名Jules Pierre Verreaux，為19世紀著名的法國植物學家、鳥類學家、自然史標本蒐藏家與交易家，曾在中國與南非工作過。  ##--SPLIT--##    而根據當地人（the natives）所說，這些豬成年後仍帶有條紋，讓我以為牠們是霍奇森（Hodgson）所描述的第二種姬豬屬物種。但是，我最近收到了四隻活的野豬，從牠們的外觀來看，應該是5個月到6個月大。這是我依據當地人（country-people）在3月和4月將牠們初給我時的狀況來估算。四隻豬中有兩隻鬱鬱寡歡，後來死了。第三隻將自己撞死，第四隻則非常難以控制，最後越來越虛弱，我不得不殺了牠。從牠們發出的咕嚕聲、尖叫聲和其他聲音來看，牠們百分之百就是豬。最大的個體，從吻鼻部到臀部大約長21/2 英呎，肩高約1 1/2 英呎。尾長23/4 英吋，末端的毛髮長 3/4 英吋。牙齒和頭骨都尚未發育完全的狀況，一般來說表示這些個體都還是幼體，由此可知成年的動物應該至少有牠們的兩倍大。牠們身上那些獨特的條紋消失了，門牙就像典型的豬一樣向外突出。 因此，牠們在之前才會被我誤認為是姬豬屬，但將牠們歸於真正的豬屬（Sus）應該更恰當。我有三件毛皮標本和三件頭骨標本。體背整體而言為黃褐色，混有濃密的黑毛，使毛皮呈現斑駁的外觀。頸後和臀部上的黑毛最長。腹側和尾巴是白色的，或多或少有黑毛混生。下巴為很深的暗褐色，有白色三角形斑塊，自嘴角向外延伸，在每側都有一排黑毛。最後一個特徵使我想起了日本野豬 （Sus leucomystax） 的特有特徵，如果中國家豬確實是由野生族群馴化而來的話，這些個體確實有可能就是該物種，也可能屬於中國南部的野豬。這些動物的外觀毫無疑問會隨著年齡增長而改變，但是牠們身上可能也具有足夠的特徵顯示牠們屬於哪種物種。我身上沒有「日本動物誌（Fauna Japan）」可以參考。我將標本寄回家鄉，讓您去解決牠們是否屬於野生日本豬的問題。我最後可能會順利獲得一隻成年個體，或許還能將活體帶回英國給倫敦動物協會的花園。我沒有豬的頭骨可以拿來跟我們的物種比較。但是，您在《皇家學報》的一篇論文中，有一張野豬中歐亞種（Sus scrofa ferus）和中歐野豬（Sus pliciceps）的圖。臺灣野豬（Sus taivanus）的頭骨前額圓滑，頂骨和顳骨的側面都是凸出而非凹下，因此可以立即就可以辨別出這兩種物種之間的差異。但是，當然不需要與野豬（Sus scrofa）做比較，因為從毛皮外觀來看，我們的豬絲毫不可能跟該物種有關。

plans.name: 19世紀西方台灣書寫史料翻譯整理計畫
plans.description:
本計畫翻譯法文史料與英文史料。法文史料《法軍遠征福爾摩沙1884-1885》作者手稿及其附錄《法軍遠征福爾摩沙1884-1885 》地圖手繪稿，為法國軍官嘉諾（Eugène Germain Garnot）紀錄清法戰爭期間在雞籠、淡水、澎湖等地的見聞。此書為清法戰爭現存最詳盡的西文資料，1894年曾於法國出版，1960年由臺灣銀行經濟研究室出版中譯本《法軍侵台始末》，是研究清法戰爭臺灣之役的重要參考。此外，英文史料為臺灣自然史相關論文30篇，是英國博物學家史溫侯（Robert Swinhoe, 1836-1877，亦翻譯為郇和、斯文豪、士委諾）在19世紀中葉擔任英國駐臺領事期間所撰寫的，紀錄臺灣的自然生態與物產，是當時西方世界了解臺灣的重要資料。另外本案包含史溫侯所撰臺灣相關文章清單1份，是費德廉老師所整理的。

完成年份：2020年

計畫執行團隊
內容力有限公司
